content.body: In physics, 'work' has a precise meaning. Work is done when a force moves an object in the direction of the force. Energy is the capacity to do work. Energy can neither be created nor destroyed — only converted from one form to another (Law of Conservation of Energy).

Work Done:
W = F × d × cos θ
W = work (Joules, J)
F = force (Newtons, N)
d = displacement in direction of force (metres, m)
θ = angle between force and displacement

If force and displacement are in SAME direction: θ=0, cos0=1
W = F × d (simplest case)

Types of Energy:
Kinetic Energy (KE): energy of motion
KE = ½mv²  (m=mass in kg, v=speed in m/s)

Gravitational Potential Energy (GPE): energy due to height
GPE = mgh  (m=mass, g=9.8 m/s², h=height in m)

Elastic PE: energy stored in stretched/compressed objects
Chemical, thermal, nuclear, electromagnetic, sound...

content.title: Energy Calculations
content.body:
A 2 kg ball is lifted 3 m high:
GPE = mgh = 2 × 9.8 × 3 = 58.8 J

The ball is released and falls. At the bottom:
All GPE converts to KE (ignoring air resistance)
KE = 58.8 J
½mv² = 58.8 → v² = 58.8 × 2/2 = 58.8 → v = 7.67 m/s

Conservation of Mechanical Energy:
KE + GPE = constant (no friction)

At top: v=0, KE=0, all energy is GPE
At bottom: h=0, GPE=0, all energy is KE

Work-Energy Theorem:
Net work done on object = Change in kinetic energy
W = ΔKE = ½mv² − ½mu²

questions.question: Work done = F × d. Unit of work is:
questions.explanation: Work is measured in Joules (J). 1 Joule = 1 Newton × 1 metre.

questions.question: Force = 20N, displacement = 5m (same direction). Work done = ?
KE = ½mv². Object m=4kg, v=3m/s. KE = ?
GPE = mgh. m=5kg, h=10m, g=9.8m/s². GPE = ?
A ball falls from 5m. At bottom, speed is (g=10):
questions.explanation: GPE→KE: mgh=½mv² → gh=½v² → v²=2×10×5=100 → v=10 m/s.

questions.question: When you carry a box horizontally (no vertical movement), work done against gravity is:
questions.explanation: Work = F×d×cosθ. For gravity (vertical), horizontal displacement means θ=90°. cos90°=0. No work done against gravity.

questions.question: Energy is measured in:
questions.explanation: Energy (like work) is measured in Joules (J).

questions.question: A stretched spring has:
questions.explanation: A stretched or compressed spring stores elastic potential energy.

questions.question: As an object falls freely, what happens to its KE and GPE?
questions.explanation: As height decreases, GPE converts to KE. Total mechanical energy stays constant.

questions.question: No work is done when:
options: The force and displacement are perpendicular
questions.answer: The force and displacement are perpendicular
questions.explanation: W = Fdcosθ. When θ=90°, cos90°=0, so W=0. No work is done.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
POWER:
Power = Work done / Time taken
P = W/t = F×v
Unit: Watt (W). 1W = 1 J/s
1 kW = 1000 W
1 MW = 1,000,000 W

Power tells us HOW FAST energy is transferred or work is done.

content.title: Power Calculations
content.body:
A motor does 6000 J of work in 30 seconds. Find its power.
P = W/t = 6000/30 = 200 W

A car engine produces force 2000N and travels at 20 m/s. Find power.
P = F×v = 2000 × 20 = 40,000 W = 40 kW

EFFICIENCY:
Efficiency = (Useful energy output / Total energy input) × 100%

No machine is 100% efficient — energy is always lost, usually as heat and sound.

Efficiency tells us what fraction of input energy is usefully converted.

content.title: Efficiency Calculations
content.body:
A light bulb receives 100 J of electrical energy.
It produces 15 J of light and 85 J of heat.
Efficiency = (15/100) × 100% = 15%

A motor is 80% efficient and receives 500 J.
Useful work = 80% × 500 = 400 J
Wasted energy = 100 J (as heat/sound)

Energy sources:
Renewable: solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, tidal — naturally replenished
Non-renewable: coal, oil, natural gas, nuclear fuel — limited supply, cause pollution
Uganda's energy: mainly hydroelectric (Owen Falls/Jinja, Karuma, Isimba dams on River Nile)

questions.question: Power is measured in:
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 Watt = 1 Joule per second.

questions.question: W=1500J done in 5s. Find power.
1 kW = ?
questions.explanation: kilo = 1000. 1 kilowatt = 1000 watts.

questions.question: Efficiency = (useful output / total input) × 100%. Motor: input=800J, useful output=600J. Efficiency = ?
Why is no machine 100% efficient?
options: Energy is always lost, usually as heat or sound
questions.answer: Energy is always lost, usually as heat or sound
questions.explanation: Friction and air resistance convert some input energy to heat and sound — this energy cannot be usefully recovered.

questions.question: A car engine force=3000N at v=25m/s. Power = ?
Solar panels are a renewable energy source because:
options: Sunlight is naturally and continuously replenished
questions.answer: Sunlight is naturally and continuously replenished
questions.explanation: Renewable energy comes from sources that are naturally replenished — the sun keeps shining.

questions.question: Uganda's largest energy source is:
questions.explanation: Uganda generates most of its electricity from hydroelectric dams on the River Nile, including Nalubaale (Owen Falls) and Karuma.

questions.question: Motor is 70% efficient. Input=1000J. Wasted energy = ?
A person climbs 10m in 20s. Mass=60kg, g=10. Power output = ?

content.body:
Work done:
W = Fd cosθ
W = work (J), F = force (N), d = displacement (m), θ = angle between force and displacement
If force parallel to motion: W = Fd
Unit: joule (J) = N·m

Power:
P = W/t = Fd/t = Fv
P = power (W), W = work (J), t = time (s)
Unit: watt (W) = J/s
1 kW = 1000 W, 1 MW = 10⁶ W

Efficiency:
Efficiency = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%
Or: Efficiency = (useful power output / total power input) × 100%
No machine is 100% efficient — some energy always lost as heat

content.title: Power Calculation
content.body:
A motor lifts a 200 kg load 5 m in 10 seconds.
Work done = mgh = 200×10×5 = 10,000 J
Power = W/t = 10,000/10 = 1000 W = 1 kW

questions.question: Work done = Force × ...
questions.explanation: W = Fd cosθ. When force and motion are parallel (θ=0), W = Fd.

questions.question: No work is done when...
options: Object moves in direction of force
questions.explanation: W = Fd cosθ. When θ=90°, cos90°=0, W=0. No work done when force ⊥ displacement.

questions.question: Power =
questions.explanation: Power = rate of doing work = Work ÷ time. Unit: watt (W).

questions.question: A 500 W motor runs for 20 s. Work done =
questions.explanation: Work = Power × time = 500 × 20 = 10,000 J

questions.question: A machine uses 2000 J but only 1500 J is useful. Efficiency =
Why is no machine 100% efficient?
options: Some energy always lost as heat (friction)
questions.answer: Some energy always lost as heat (friction)
questions.explanation: Friction in moving parts always converts some energy to heat, reducing useful output.

questions.question: Unit of work done is...
questions.explanation: 1 joule = work done when 1 N force moves object 1 m in direction of force.

questions.question: A person of mass 60 kg climbs 3 m stairs. Work against gravity (g=10 N/kg) =
A car engine outputs 40 kW and moves at 20 m/s. Driving force =
Watt is equivalent to...
questions.explanation: 1 W = 1 J/s (one joule per second). Power measures rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?

content.body: Energy resources are used to generate electricity. They can be classified as renewable (replenished naturally) or non-renewable (finite supply that will run out).

content.title: Non-Renewable Resources
content.body:
Coal, oil, natural gas (fossil fuels) and nuclear fuel.
Advantages: reliable, high energy density, available on demand
Disadvantages: CO₂ emissions (fossil fuels), finite supply, environmental damage

Nuclear power: no CO₂, but radioactive waste and safety concerns

content.title: Renewable Resources
content.body:
Solar: panels convert sunlight to electricity. No pollution. Intermittent.
Wind: turbines convert kinetic energy. No pollution. Intermittent.
Hydroelectric: falling water drives turbines. Reliable. Environmental impact on rivers.
Tidal/Wave: ocean energy. Predictable but limited locations.
Geothermal: Earth's internal heat. Very reliable. Limited locations.

Electricity generation:
Generator: converts kinetic energy → electrical energy (electromagnetic induction)
Transformer: changes voltage for efficient transmission
High voltage transmission reduces current → less energy lost as heat (P=I²R)

questions.question: Which is a renewable energy source?
questions.explanation: Wind is continuously replenished by natural processes. Coal, oil, and natural gas are finite fossil fuels.

questions.question: Fossil fuels release CO₂ when burned. This contributes to...
questions.explanation: CO₂ is a greenhouse gas that traps heat in the atmosphere, contributing to global warming and climate change.

questions.question: Solar panels convert...
questions.explanation: Solar photovoltaic (PV) panels convert light energy directly to electrical energy using the photoelectric effect.

questions.question: Hydroelectric power uses...
options: Kinetic energy of falling water to drive turbines
questions.answer: Kinetic energy of falling water to drive turbines
questions.explanation: In hydroelectric power, water falls from high reservoir, its kinetic energy turns turbines, which drive generators.

questions.question: Why is electricity transmitted at high voltage?
options: To reduce current and minimise heat energy losses
questions.answer: To reduce current and minimise heat energy losses
questions.explanation: Power loss = I²R. High voltage means low current, so less power lost as heat in cables.

questions.question: Nuclear power produces...
questions.explanation: Nuclear fission releases energy without burning fuel, so no CO₂. But it produces radioactive waste requiring careful disposal.

questions.question: Which energy source is most reliable regardless of weather?
questions.explanation: Nuclear power plants generate electricity continuously regardless of weather. Solar and wind are intermittent.

questions.question: A generator converts...
questions.explanation: A generator uses electromagnetic induction: rotating coil in magnetic field → electrical current.

questions.question: Geothermal energy comes from...
questions.explanation: Geothermal energy harnesses heat from within the Earth (from radioactive decay and original formation).

questions.question: The main disadvantage of wind power is...
options: It is intermittent (only works when wind blows)
questions.answer: It is intermittent (only works when wind blows)
questions.explanation: Wind turbines only generate electricity when wind blows at suitable speed. Energy storage is needed for calm periods.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Work, Energy & Power. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Work, Energy & Power concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Work, Energy & Power builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Work, Energy & Power. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Work, Energy & Power. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Work, Energy & Power include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Work, Energy & Power. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
